titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 31 May 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: Though I have not heard from you since I wrote last, neither have we any novelties greatly worth the writing, yet being to go out of towne to morrow towards Askot, me thinckes I must needs bid you farewell.

Your frend EdmondsThomas Edmondes (knighted, 1603). came out of Fraunce the last weeke and bringes worde that the peace there is so forward that yt lackes nothing but the publishinge,The peace was publicly proclaimed May 26. which is thought to be performed by this time, and all the townes upon the point of deliverie. He is like to be tossed to and fro and brickewaldCorruption of French bricoler, associated by popular etymology with the brick wall of the tennis court. like a tennis ball from the one side to the other somwhat to be concluded: for we have two moneths time to deliberate whether we will treat or no, and three moneths more for the treaty: wherupon we are not yet fully resolved but consult dayly about yt and the further they looke into yt the more difficulties appeare, insomuch that the wisest judgements are distracted and know not what to choose; for mine owne part having heard what hath bin sayde pro et contra on both sides, I cannot well discerne whether were better an insidious peace, or so faint and weake a warre as we have made hitherto and are like to do hereafter, which doth rather prolonge and drawe on the consumption then remedie the disease. In the meane time the state of Ireland stands in yll termes, for we are so wholly possest with this ymaginarie peace that we cannot attend yt. Not past eight dayes since it was decreed that Sir Rich: Bingham, Sir Samuell Bagnoll and Sir Henry Docwray shold be sent thether with ech of them a regiment of 2000: but that course is altered and now they talke that Sir W. Raleigh and Sir George CarewOf Clopton, Warwickshire. He was created Lord Carew of Clopton in 1605, and Earl of Totnes in 1626. shold undertake yt, but how longe that will hold is uncertain.

ZouchEdward, eleventh Lord Zouche of Harringworth, and Christopher Perkins (knighted, 1603). with Dr. Parkins is going ambassador into Denmarke with certain secret instructions to go into Poland likewise about a Dutch baron. The newes I wrote of Sir Charles Blunt must be recalled for yt was a mistaking of a combat that passed betwene a Frenchman and a Dutchman that was knighted at Cales. Bluet in Britain is sayde to be rased. One CaryGeorge Carew (knighted, 1603), son of Thomas Carew of Antony, and secretary to Sir Thomas Egerton. is appointed secretarie to the Lord Keper for such busines. Sir Fra: Vere is to go very shortly to the States. The Lord bishop of GlocesterJohn Bullingham. is lately dead. All your private frends are as you left them, and so wishing the continuance of your health and welfare I commit you to God.

closer.dateline: From London this last of May 1598.

address.addrLine: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.

div.head: Editor's Note
div.p: Written from London on 31 May 1598, the day before Chamberlain was to leave for Ascott, this letter offers his most sustained meditation on the question of Anglo-Spanish peace. Thomas Edmondes has returned from France reporting the peace all but concluded (it was proclaimed on May 26), but Chamberlain is genuinely uncertain whether peace is better than the half-hearted war England has waged. The letter's central passage weighs the risks: Dutch commercial exclusion, the worsening situation in Ireland, and the general paralysis of English policy. He reports that the original plan to send Bingham, Bagnall, and Docwra to Ireland with regiments has been scrapped in favour of an alternative involving Raleigh and Sir George Carew, which he expects will also come to nothing. A final miscellany covers the Zouche-Perkins embassy to Denmark and Poland, the correction of Chamberlain's earlier report about Sir Charles Blount's duel (which was actually a different fight altogether), the reported demolition of the Spanish fort at Blavet in Brittany, a new appointment as secretary to the Lord Keeper, Sir Francis Vere's imminent departure for the States, and the death of the Bishop of Gloucester.
